choices.A: Identify the projection bottleneck (I/O, CPU, or event deserialization). Deploy a fix to the projection logic. Run a catch-up projection in parallel to close the 30s gap. Once caught up, swap the stale view for the fresh one atomically.
choices.B: Rebuild the materialized view from scratch by replaying all events
choices.C: Pause writes until the projection catches up
choices.D: Increase the read timeout to 30 seconds so users wait for fresh data

questions.explanation: Step 1: Diagnose why the projection is lagging â€” is it a code bug, resource bottleneck, or schema inefficiency? Step 2: Fix the root cause in the projection logic. Step 3: A parallel catch-up projection replays the 30s backlog without affecting the live system. Step 4: Atomic swap replaces the stale view with the fresh one, providing consistency without downtime. Pausing writes or full replay are unnecessarily disruptive.

questions.question: An orchestrator manages 8 agents with a shared state store. Agent A reads state, spends 5 seconds processing, then writes. Agent B reads the same state, spends 2 seconds processing, then writes. Both read at time T=0. Agent B writes at T=2. Agent A writes at T=5, overwriting B's changes (lost update). The system uses no concurrency control. What layered solution prevents lost updates while minimizing contention?

choices.A: Add a global lock â€” all reads and writes acquire an exclusive lock
choices.B: Use optimistic concurrency with version numbers. If A's write detects the version has changed (B already wrote), A must re-read, re-process incorporating B's changes, and retry. For frequently-contended keys, add exponential backoff.
choices.C: Let last-write-wins and accept occasional data loss
choices.D: Restrict all agents to read-only access

questions.explanation: Step 1: A global lock serializes all access, destroying throughput. Step 2: Optimistic concurrency allows parallel reads (common case) and detects conflicts only at write time (rare case). Step 3: When A's write is rejected due to version mismatch, A re-reads B's updated state, reprocesses, and retries. Step 4: For hot keys with frequent conflicts, exponential backoff prevents retry storms. This balances correctness with throughput.

questions.question: A capacity planning analysis shows: current task arrival rate is 100/hour, growing 10% monthly. Current processing capacity is 150/hour with 5 agents. Each new agent adds 25 tasks/hour of capacity but also adds 5 tasks/hour of coordination overhead (reducing effective gain to 20 tasks/hour). At what month will the system need its first capacity expansion, and how many agents should be added?

choices.A: Month 5 (arrival rate ~161/hour), add 1 agent (capacity -> 170/hour)
choices.B: Month 4 (arrival rate ~146/hour), add 1 agent (capacity -> 170/hour), providing headroom until month 6
choices.C: Month 3 (arrival rate ~133/hour), add 2 agents for safety
choices.D: No expansion needed within 6 months

questions.explanation: Step 1: Monthly arrival rates: M1=110, M2=121, M3=133, M4=146, M5=161. Step 2: Current capacity = 150/hour. At month 4 (146/hour), utilization hits 97% â€” dangerously close to saturation. Step 3: Add 1 agent: +25 raw - 5 overhead = +20 effective, new capacity = 170/hour. Step 4: With 170/hour capacity, M5 (161/hour) = 95% utilization. Month 6 (~177/hour) would require another agent. Adding at month 4 gives one month of buffer before hitting capacity, which is prudent.

questions.question: A complex workflow has a nested sub-workflow structure: Main workflow W calls sub-workflow S1 at step 3 and sub-workflow S2 at step 5. S1 has 4 steps, S2 has 3 steps. Step 2 of S1 fails. The error propagation policy is: sub-workflows can retry internally up to 2 times, then must propagate failure to the parent. The parent can retry the entire sub-workflow once, then must execute compensations. How many total executions of S1's step 2 are possible before compensations begin?

choices.A: 3 executions (original + 2 retries)
choices.B: 6 executions: S1 tries step 2 three times (original + 2 retries), fails, parent retries S1, S1 again tries step 2 three times (original + 2 retries)
choices.C: 4 executions (2 internal retries + 1 parent retry + 1 final attempt)
choices.D: 9 executions (3 attempts * 3 parent retries)

questions.explanation: Step 1: S1's internal retry policy: original execution + 2 retries = 3 attempts. All fail. Step 2: S1 propagates failure to parent W. Step 3: W retries the entire sub-workflow S1 (1 retry). Step 4: S1 restarts and again tries step 2: original + 2 retries = 3 attempts. All fail. Step 5: S1 propagates failure again. W has exhausted its 1 retry. Compensations begin. Total: 3 + 3 = 6 executions of step 2.

questions.question: A dependency graph has a diamond pattern: Task A fans out to B and C, both of which fan in to D. Task B takes 10 minutes. Task C takes 3 minutes. The orchestrator detects that C finished 7 minutes before B. During those 7 minutes, C's output could become stale if external data changes. What orchestration pattern addresses this staleness risk?

choices.A: Cache C's output and use it regardless â€” staleness is acceptable
choices.B: Delay C's execution to start 7 minutes after B starts, so both finish near-simultaneously and D receives fresh inputs from both
choices.C: Execute C twice â€” once immediately (early result) and once just before D starts (fresh validation). Use the second result if it differs from the first.
choices.D: Execute B and C simultaneously but add a freshness check: when B completes, verify C's output is still valid. If stale, re-run C. Then proceed to D.

questions.explanation: Step 1: C finishes 7 minutes before B, creating a staleness window. Step 2: Delaying C (option B) wastes 7 minutes of potential parallelism if the data does not change. Step 3: A freshness check after B completes (option D) exploits full parallelism (C runs immediately) and only re-runs C if its output is actually stale. This is optimal: fast path when data is stable, correct path when data changes, and no wasted time.

questions.question: A multi-agent pipeline processes customer orders. The orchestrator must ensure exactly-once processing semantics. The pipeline has 3 stages: validate, process, confirm. A network hiccup causes the 'confirm' acknowledgment to be lost. The orchestrator retries the entire pipeline. Without safeguards, the customer is charged twice. What architecture prevents this?

choices.A: Assign each order a unique idempotency key. Each stage checks if the key has already been processed before executing. The 'process' stage uses the idempotency key to make the charge operation safe to retry â€” a second charge attempt with the same key is rejected by the payment provider.
choices.B: Use at-most-once delivery to prevent duplicates
choices.C: Increase the network timeout to prevent hiccups
choices.D: Add a manual review step before each charge

questions.explanation: Step 1: The lost ACK means the orchestrator does not know if 'confirm' succeeded â€” it must retry to avoid lost orders. Step 2: Without idempotency, retry causes duplicate processing. Step 3: An idempotency key (e.g., order ID + timestamp) allows each stage to check 'have I already processed this?' Step 4: The payment provider uses the key to deduplicate charges â€” the second charge attempt returns success without actually charging. This achieves exactly-once semantics through at-least-once delivery + idempotent operations.

questions.question: An orchestrator conducts a 5-agent roundtable to decide between migrating to a new framework (high risk, high reward) vs. incrementally improving the existing one (low risk, moderate reward). Three agents favor migration. Two agents favor incremental improvement. The facilitator asks each migration supporter to identify the single biggest risk. All three name different risks (data loss, downtime, skill gap). The improvement supporters identify two advantages (stability, team familiarity). What synthesis approach produces the most robust decision?

choices.A: Construct a risk-reward matrix: evaluate whether each identified migration risk can be mitigated to an acceptable level. If all three risks have viable mitigations with bounded cost, migration is justified. If any risk is unmitigable, the incremental approach or a phased migration is recommended.
choices.B: Go with incremental improvement since the risks of migration are diverse and numerous
choices.C: Go with migration since more agents support it and the rewards are higher
choices.D: Conduct a second roundtable with different agents

questions.explanation: Step 1: Three different risks from three supporters suggests the migration has broad risk surface, not one focused concern. Step 2: Simply counting votes ignores risk analysis depth. Step 3: A risk-reward matrix evaluates each risk's mitigability and cost. Step 4: If mitigations exist and their combined cost does not erode the reward, migration is justified. If any risk is unmitigable (e.g., data loss with no rollback plan), the safer incremental path or a phased approach is appropriate. This evidence-based synthesis outperforms both raw voting and blanket risk aversion.

questions.question: An orchestrator manages a complex DAG of 12 tasks across 4 agent pools. Mid-execution, 3 tasks have completed (T1, T2, T3), 2 are running (T4, T5), and 7 are pending. A priority override arrives: new critical task T13 must execute immediately. T13 requires the same agent pool currently running T5 (single agent in that pool). T5 is 80% complete and has no checkpoint. T4 depends on T5's output. T6 and T7 depend on T4. What is the optimal orchestration sequence?

choices.A: Pre-empt T5 immediately, losing 80% of its work. Run T13. Then re-run T5 from scratch.
choices.B: Queue T13 to run after T5 completes (estimated 20% remaining time). Accept the delay to T13.
choices.C: Cancel T5 and all its dependents (T4, T6, T7), run T13, then rebuild the dependency chain
choices.D: Evaluate T13's urgency against T5's sunk cost: if T13's deadline allows a short wait (T5's remaining 20%), let T5 finish and immediately run T13. If T13 cannot wait, pre-empt T5, run T13, then re-run T5. In either case, update T4/T6/T7 estimated start times and notify dependent tasks of the delay.

questions.explanation: Step 1: T5 is 80% complete with no checkpoint â€” pre-emption loses significant work. Step 2: T13 is critical but 'immediately' has varying interpretations â€” a 20% remaining T5 might finish in minutes. Step 3: The orchestrator must evaluate the cost-benefit: (cost of T5 restart + downstream delays) vs (cost of short T13 delay). Step 4: If T13's deadline permits a brief wait, preserving T5's work is optimal. If not, pre-emption is necessary. Step 5: Either way, all dependent tasks (T4->T6->T7) need their schedules updated and stakeholders notified of cascade impact.
